_meta.description: Full ResearchResult for business question q_nm26_repeat_purchase

problem.title: How can we improve repeat purchase for NutriMix?
problem.context: Nutrimix gets healthy initial adoption but repeat behavior is inconsistent. We need to identify which levers create an enduring household habit.

causal_statements.statement: Personas with Health Worry Level above 0.62 are 2.41x more likely to adopt than those below 0.62, because higher health worry level increases need recognition earlier in the funnel.

Within City Classification = Tier1, personas with Health Worry Level above 0.62 are 3.17x more likely to adopt than peers below 0.62, because higher health worry level increases need recognition earlier in the funnel.
Personas with Price Sensitivity above 0.55 are 1.87x less likely to adopt than those below 0.55, because higher price sensitivity makes price friction show up faster at purchase.
Personas with Open to New Brands above 0.58 are 1.94x more likely to adopt than those below 0.58, because higher open to new brands pushes the modeled decision scores upward.

hypothesis_verdicts.evidence_summary: Personas with medical_authority_trust > 0.60 adopt at 2.3x the rate of those below the threshold. Pediatrician endorsement produced the largest single awareness boost (+0.15) observed across interventions.
recommended_actions: Scale pediatrician co-marketing materials with clinical language
Create a 'recommended by your doctor' QR-code onboarding flow

hypothesis_verdicts.evidence_summary: Reducing price from ₹599 to ₹499 improved adoption by 4.2pp. Effort reduction (0.3→0.2) added a further 2.1pp. Combined effect is meaningful but secondary to trust signals.
recommended_actions: Test a ₹499 introductory trial pack with single-serve sachets
Offer same-day delivery via quick commerce for re-order convenience

tree_synthesis.synthesis_narrative: The single strongest lever for first-trial conversion is medical authority trust, which activates both awareness and consideration pathways. Price and friction matter but are secondary — they affect purchase-stage drop-offs rather than blocking consideration. The highest-leverage intervention is a pediatrician-led endorsement programme that front-loads clinical credibility before the consideration stage. Price optimisation should be tested in Tier 2 markets specifically, where budget consciousness scores average 0.12 points higher.
recommended_actions: Launch a Paediatrician Partner Programme with co-branded materials
Introduce a ₹499 trial pack for Tier 2 first-time buyers
A/B test 'per-day cost' anchor messaging (₹20/day) against standard pricing display

executive_summary.headline: NutriMix trial conversion is gated by trust, not awareness — the paediatrician channel is the highest-leverage unlock.
executive_summary.trajectory_summary: Over the 6-month simulation, 15.5% of households adopted NutriMix on first exposure. Consideration-stage drop-off accounts for 36% of all rejections, driven by insufficient research time and brand unfamiliarity. Households that passed consideration converted at 45%.
key_drivers: Health anxiety and immunity concern drive need recognition — high scorers convert at 2.4x average
Openness to indie brands is the key consideration gate for an unfamiliar brand like LittleJoys
Budget consciousness caps purchase conversion at the higher Tier 2 price reference point
recommendations: Invest in paediatrician co-marketing to move trust-sensitive households through consideration
Introduce a ₹499 introductory trial pack for Tier 2 price-sensitive cohorts
Create ingredient-story content (video + label) to satisfy high-research-tendency parents
risk_factors: If brand awareness does not scale alongside trust investment, reach caps well below category potential
Taste rejection from children (child_taste_veto mean 0.53 for 3-6 age band) is a post-trial churn risk

probe_tree_excerpt.question_template: From your perspective, what is the strongest signal for: what drives first-time trial among health-anxious parents?

result.evidence_summary: 12 of 15 adopter interviews cited paediatrician recommendation or 'doctor-recommended' labelling as the tipping point for trial. Non-adopters with high health anxiety described needing 'clinical proof, not just Instagram claims'.

interview_responses.content: My paediatrician mentioned clean-label nutrition supplements at our last visit. I wasn't specifically looking for NutriMix but it came up in my Google search right after. The 'paediatrician approved' badge on the pack made me add it to cart immediately.

I've seen NutriMix on Instagram but I need to know what actual doctors say before I give anything like this to my daughter. The influencer posts feel paid. If my daughter's paediatrician recommended it at our next appointment, I'd probably try it.

response_clusters.description: Parents who require explicit clinical or paediatrician endorsement before trialling any supplement. Trust is a prerequisite, not a bonus.
representative_quotes: My paediatrician's word matters more than any ad I see
I only buy what the doctor says is safe

response_clusters.description: Parents who self-research extensively but convert once evidence aligns across multiple sources (reviews, doctors, peers).
representative_quotes: I read every Amazon review and cross-checked the ingredients list
Three different sources said the same thing so I felt confident

response_clusters.description: High health anxiety but low trust in the brand. These parents recognise the need but cannot resolve the trust gap without a stronger authority signal.
representative_quotes: I worry about what's in these products
I'd rather give real food than risk an unknown supplement
